findings.title: Unauthenticated Stored XSS via Form Submission (HTML field type)
findings.nonce_required: wpr-addons-js (public nonce, available in page source)
findings.details:
The add_to_submissions() handler accepts form_content from POST. When the field type is 'html', the wpr_sanitize_form_field() method at line 178 uses wp_kses_post() which permits a rich set of HTML tags including , ,
, and other XSS vectors. The sanitized value is stored as post_meta on a 'wpr_submissions' CPT. When an admin views submissions in wp-admin, the stored payload executes in admin context. The nonce 'wpr-addons-js' is publicly available via wp_localize_script on every frontend page. Premium code check (wpr_fs()->can_use_premium_code()) gates the handler, reducing scope to premium users only.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Taxonomy Name Injection in AJAX Search
findings.details: The data_fetch() AJAX handler uses $_POST['wpr_option_post_type'] and $_POST['wpr_taxonomy_type'] directly as taxonomy names in tax_query arrays without sanitization or validation against registered taxonomies. An attacker can supply arbitrary taxonomy names to enumerate whether private/internal taxonomies exist and potentially probe for posts tagged with private taxonomy terms. Additionally, $_POST['wpr_query_type'] is used to set post_type in WP_Query with only sanitize_text_field applied (line 130/142) -- this allows querying non-public post types like 'shop_coupon' or 'flamingo_inbound' when combined with premium code bypass. The query results return post titles and permalinks, enabling information disclosure of non-public content.

findings.title: Unauthenticated Arbitrary Method Call via REST API Dispatch
findings.details: The REST route callback at line 24-25 dispatches to $this->{$request['action']}($request) where {action} is fully attacker-controlled via the URL path. While all defined handler methods check current_user_can('edit_posts'), this pattern allows calling any public method on the class including inherited methods from stdClass/Object. Calling non-existent methods causes fatal errors (potential DoS). Currently mitigated by individual capability checks in each handler method, but the insecure dispatch pattern is a ticking time bomb -- adding any new method without a capability check would create an unauthenticated vulnerability. This is a defense-in-depth issue.

false_positive_summary.file_upload_handler: Well-implemented with HMAC-signed upload tokens, rate limiting, MIME validation, extension whitelist+blacklist, .htaccess hardening. Nonce is public but the HMAC token is properly bound to the form render context. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.woocommerce_wishlist_compare_handlers: All wishlist/compare/count handlers (add_to_wishlist, remove_from_wishlist, add_to_compare, remove_from_compare, count_wishlist_items, count_compare_items, update_mini_wishlist, update_mini_compare) operate on cookie-based lists without writing to database in exploitable ways. Standard WooCommerce frontend functionality. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.form_email_handler: Uses public nonce but email recipients, subjects, and content templates come from get_option() (admin-configured), not from user input. Form content goes into the email body but this is expected contact-form behavior. The email is sent to admin-configured addresses. NOT exploitable for email injection -- headers use admin-set options.
false_positive_summary.form_webhook_handler: Webhook URL comes from get_option(), not user input. Body content is form data, which is expected. NOT exploitable for SSRF.
false_positive_summary.grid_filters_ajax: Uses public nonce, accepts grid_settings from POST for WP_Query. Has is_allowed_grid_query_post_type() check that restricts unauthenticated users to publicly_queryable post types. Properly mitigated.
false_positive_summary.post_likes_handler: Uses a separate nonce (wpr-post-likes-nonce). Properly validates post_id as numeric. Stores IP/user associations in post meta. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.compare_popup_action: wpr_get_page_content uses public nonce, but checks post_password_required() and requires 'publish' status. Only renders published post content through Elementor. NOT exploitable beyond viewing published content.
false_positive_summary.custom_meta_keys: Uses wpr-addons-editor-js nonce (editor-only, not public). NOT exploitable for unauth.
false_positive_summary.mailchimp_subscribe: Standard subscription handler. API key from server options. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.password_protected_content: Uses separate nonce (wpr_ppc_verify), compares against stored password in Elementor widget data. Cookie-based auth. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.widget_builder_endpoints: All REST routes require manage_options capability. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.mega_menu_rest: Validates item_id, checks post type, checks publish status, checks user capability for non-published items. NOT exploitable.
false_positive_summary.form_actions_status: Uses submission_secret (64-byte random token) for authorization. Properly protected against enumeration.
